APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses neural dialogue agents and reinforcement learning for information access—topics squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues. It proposes a novel differentiable Soft-KB lookup and trains end-to-end agents (GRU belief trackers + REINFORCE policy), evaluated against reasonable baselines (rule, Hard-KB, No-KB) in simulations and with real users on a Movies-KB corpus, matching expected methods and evaluation standards. The problem formulation, use of neural/RL techniques, and positioning within dialogue/IA research make it highly relevant to the venue audience. | B(5): Yes — the paper addresses core NLP/ML topics (dialogue systems for information access and reinforcement learning) and proposes a novel differentiable Soft-KB lookup relevant to both communities. The methodology (neural belief trackers, GRU policy trained with REINFORCE, end-to-end training from user feedback), datasets (various Movies-KB splits), and evaluations (success rate, rank-based episodic reward, comparisons to rule, hard-KB, and no-KB baselines, simulated and real-user tests) match expectations for a top venue. The problem formulation and positioning (replacing symbolic KB queries to enable end-to-end RL) are appropriate and clearly targeted to the intended NLP/ML audience.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses neural dialogue agents and reinforcement learning for information access—topics squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues. It proposes a novel differentiable Soft-KB lookup and trains end-to-end agents (GRU belief trackers + REINFORCE policy), evaluated against reasonable baselines (rule, Hard-KB, No-KB) in simulations and with real users on a Movies-KB corpus, matching expected methods and evaluation standards. The problem formulation, use of neural/RL techniques, and positioning within dialogue/IA research make it highly relevant to the venue audience.

reviewer_b.justification: Yes — the paper addresses core NLP/ML topics (dialogue systems for information access and reinforcement learning) and proposes a novel differentiable Soft-KB lookup relevant to both communities. The methodology (neural belief trackers, GRU policy trained with REINFORCE, end-to-end training from user feedback), datasets (various Movies-KB splits), and evaluations (success rate, rank-based episodic reward, comparisons to rule, hard-KB, and no-KB baselines, simulated and real-user tests) match expectations for a top venue. The problem formulation and positioning (replacing symbolic KB queries to enable end-to-end RL) are appropriate and clearly targeted to the intended NLP/ML audience.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem by introducing a differentiable Soft-KB lookup that replaces symbolic/hard queries and produces a posterior over KB entities, enabling end-to-end reinforcement learning of dialogue agents. Empirical gains (higher task success and reward in simulation and with real users) and a fully neural KB-InfoBot trained from user feedback suggest the approach could change how researchers build information-access dialogue systems. However, strong modeling assumptions (independent slot values), inability to handle new entities/relations at test time, and reliance on simulated users for training limit immediate broad applicability, preventing a transformative rating. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem—making KB access differentiable to enable end-to-end reinforcement learning of dialogue agents—by introducing a Soft-KB lookup that replaces symbolic queries and produces a posterior over entities. Empirically it shows higher task success and reward in simulation and with real users, and demonstrates a fully neural KB-InfoBot trained from user feedback, which could shift how researchers design dialogue architectures. However, the strong independence assumption across slots, inability to handle new entities/relations at test time, and reliance on simulated users constrain immediate broad applicability, so the impact is strong but not fully transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem by introducing a differentiable Soft-KB lookup that replaces symbolic/hard queries and produces a posterior over KB entities, enabling end-to-end reinforcement learning of dialogue agents. Empirical gains (higher task success and reward in simulation and with real users) and a fully neural KB-InfoBot trained from user feedback suggest the approach could change how researchers build information-access dialogue systems. However, strong modeling assumptions (independent slot values), inability to handle new entities/relations at test time, and reliance on simulated users for training limit immediate broad applicability, preventing a transformative rating.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—making KB access differentiable to enable end-to-end reinforcement learning of dialogue agents—by introducing a Soft-KB lookup that replaces symbolic queries and produces a posterior over entities. Empirically it shows higher task success and reward in simulation and with real users, and demonstrates a fully neural KB-InfoBot trained from user feedback, which could shift how researchers design dialogue architectures. However, the strong independence assumption across slots, inability to handle new entities/relations at test time, and reliance on simulated users constrain immediate broad applicability, so the impact is strong but not fully transformative.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a clearly new technical component — a differentiable Soft-KB lookup that replaces prior symbolic/hard KB queries and computes a posterior over KB rows from slot-wise beliefs — enabling end-to-end training of belief trackers and policy with reinforcement learning. This is a non-trivial methodological advance over earlier hard-lookup approaches and is shown to improve success/reward in simulation and with real users. The authors also explicitly distinguish their contribution from prior work and acknowledge limitations (e.g., independence assumptions), so the idea is notably novel though not a fundamentally new problem formulation. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new technical component—a differentiable Soft-KB lookup that computes a posterior over KB rows from slot-wise belief distributions and knowledge indicators—replacing prior symbolic/hard KB queries and enabling end-to-end online RL training of language understanding and policy. The work distinguishes itself by integrating this Soft-KB with policy learning (REINFORCE) and demonstrating improved success and rewards over Hard-KB, No-KB, and rule baselines, which indicates a non-trivial extension beyond existing methods. The authors explicitly state limitations (strong independence assumption across slots; inability to handle new entities/relations at test time), suggesting the idea is novel but bounded by modeling assumptions rather than being a trivial recombination of prior techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new technical component — a differentiable Soft-KB lookup that replaces prior symbolic/hard KB queries and computes a posterior over KB rows from slot-wise beliefs — enabling end-to-end training of belief trackers and policy with reinforcement learning. This is a non-trivial methodological advance over earlier hard-lookup approaches and is shown to improve success/reward in simulation and with real users. The authors also explicitly distinguish their contribution from prior work and acknowledge limitations (e.g., independence assumptions), so the idea is notably novel though not a fundamentally new problem formulation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new technical component—a differentiable Soft-KB lookup that computes a posterior over KB rows from slot-wise belief distributions and knowledge indicators—replacing prior symbolic/hard KB queries and enabling end-to-end online RL training of language understanding and policy. The work distinguishes itself by integrating this Soft-KB with policy learning (REINFORCE) and demonstrating improved success and rewards over Hard-KB, No-KB, and rule baselines, which indicates a non-trivial extension beyond existing methods. The authors explicitly state limitations (strong independence assumption across slots; inability to handle new entities/relations at test time), suggesting the idea is novel but bounded by modeling assumptions rather than being a trivial recombination of prior techniques.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents comprehensive experiments (simulated and real users) and compares the Soft-KB approach against several baselines/ablations (Rule, RL, No-KB, Hard-KB) with clear metrics (success, rank-based reward) and reports hyperparameters, which supports the main claims. It also evaluates different belief trackers (neural vs hand-crafted), lending further analysis of component contributions. However, important limitations remain (strong independence assumption, inability to handle new entities/relations, reliance on simulated users for training) and code availability is not mentioned, leaving minor reproducibility and generalization gaps. | B(4): The paper presents substantial experimental validation: comparisons against multiple baselines (Rule, RL, No-KB, Hard-KB), tests across several KB sizes, and evaluation both in simulation and with real users, using clear success and reward metrics. Ablations are present via different belief trackers (neural vs hand-crafted) and the No-KB/Hard-KB variants, and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility. The authors explicitly discuss key limitations (the strong slot-independence assumption, inability to handle new entities/relations, and reliance on a simulator for RL training). Missing from the summary is mention of released code and whether additional sensitivity analyses (e.g., to user simulator behavior or KB noise) were performed, hence a solid but not exhaustive evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents comprehensive experiments (simulated and real users) and compares the Soft-KB approach against several baselines/ablations (Rule, RL, No-KB, Hard-KB) with clear metrics (success, rank-based reward) and reports hyperparameters, which supports the main claims. It also evaluates different belief trackers (neural vs hand-crafted), lending further analysis of component contributions. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (clear progression from motivation to Soft-KB method, belief trackers, policy, and experiments) and states its main contributions early (introducing a differentiable Soft-KB lookup and end-to-end RL training). Notation and methodology are presented clearly enough to follow (e.g., how per-slot beliefs and q’s are marginalized to form the KB posterior), and experimental setup and metrics (success definition, reward shaping, dataset splits) are explicit. Minor presentation gaps remain — for example code availability is not mentioned and some assumptions/limitations (independence of slots, handling new entities) suggest places where additional illustrative figures or examples could improve clarity. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (Soft-KB lookup, end-to-end RL training). Notation is reasonably well-defined in the summary (e.g., per-slot beliefs ptj and knowledge indicators qtj) and the methodology, datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics are clearly described. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, with limitations (strong independence assumption, need for simulators) explicitly acknowledged, suggesting only minor presentation issues remain.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (clear progression from motivation to Soft-KB method, belief trackers, policy, and experiments) and states its main contributions early (introducing a differentiable Soft-KB lookup and end-to-end RL training). Notation and methodology are presented clearly enough to follow (e.g., how per-slot beliefs and q’s are marginalized to form the KB posterior), and experimental setup and metrics (success definition, reward shaping, dataset splits) are explicit. Minor presentation gaps remain — for example code availability is not mentioned and some assumptions/limitations (independence of slots, handling new entities) suggest places where additional illustrative figures or examples could improve clarity.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate baselines (hand-crafted rule agent, hybrid RL agent, No-KB and a Hard-KB prior-work baseline) using the same Movies-KB datasets and consistent metrics (dialogue success and episodic reward), so the experiments appear fair. It clearly analyzes the key methodological difference (Soft-KB posterior vs. symbolic/hard queries) and reports improvements over Hard-KB and other baselines, and the summary states that related work coverage is thorough. The paper also acknowledges limitations (e.g., independence assumption, need for simulators), but it does not clearly note cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there is limited explicit discussion of negative/neutral comparisons—hence minor omissions rather than major gaps. | B(4): The paper evaluates against several sensible baselines (rule-based agent, RL agent with hand-crafted belief tracker, No-KB ablation, and a Hard-KB prior method) using the same Movies-KB dataset(s) and consistent success/reward metrics, including simulations and real-user tests. It analyzes differences (Soft-KB vs. hard lookup and ablations) and explicitly states limitations (e.g., independence assumptions, handling new entities), and related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor omissions remain (no mention of comparison to other recent end-to-end KB-access approaches or public code), so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate baselines (hand-crafted rule agent, hybrid RL agent, No-KB and a Hard-KB prior-work baseline) using the same Movies-KB datasets and consistent metrics (dialogue success and episodic reward), so the experiments appear fair. It clearly analyzes the key methodological difference (Soft-KB posterior vs. symbolic/hard queries) and reports improvements over Hard-KB and other baselines, and the summary states that related work coverage is thorough. The paper also acknowledges limitations (e.g., independence assumption, need for simulators), but it does not clearly note cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there is limited explicit discussion of negative/neutral comparisons—hence minor omissions rather than major gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against several sensible baselines (rule-based agent, RL agent with hand-crafted belief tracker, No-KB ablation, and a Hard-KB prior method) using the same Movies-KB dataset(s) and consistent success/reward metrics, including simulations and real-user tests. It analyzes differences (Soft-KB vs. hard lookup and ablations) and explicitly states limitations (e.g., independence assumptions, handling new entities), and related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor omissions remain (no mention of comparison to other recent end-to-end KB-access approaches or public code), so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are generally supported: the Soft-KB lookup is well-defined (posterior obtained by marginalizing per-slot belief distributions ptj and knowledge indicators qtj) and empirical results show improved task success and reward against reasonable baselines (Rule, Hard-KB, No-KB) in simulation and with real users. The mathematical treatment appears correct given the stated assumptions, but it crucially depends on the strong and acknowledged independence assumption across slots. Experimental methodology is sound in using multiple baselines, clear reward/success metrics, and both simulated and real-user evaluation, but the summary does not report statistical significance tests and relies on simulated users for much RL training. Overall the conclusions are supported but tempered by the independence assumption and some missing experimental details. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments comparing Soft-KB agents to appropriate baselines (Rule, Hard-KB, No-KB, RL) across multiple dataset sizes and using sensible success/reward metrics, including some real-user evaluation. The mathematical formulation — computing a posterior over KB rows by marginalizing independent per-slot belief distributions — is plausible and correctly described, but relies on the strong independence assumption the authors explicitly acknowledge. The experimental methodology is generally sound (proper baselines, reward design, dataset splits), though reliance on a user simulator for RL training and no mention of statistical significance or code release are minor gaps that do not fundamentally undermine the reported conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are generally supported: the Soft-KB lookup is well-defined (posterior obtained by marginalizing per-slot belief distributions ptj and knowledge indicators qtj) and empirical results show improved task success and reward against reasonable baselines (Rule, Hard-KB, No-KB) in simulation and with real users. The mathematical treatment appears correct given the stated assumptions, but it crucially depends on the strong and acknowledged independence assumption across slots. Experimental methodology is sound in using multiple baselines, clear reward/success metrics, and both simulated and real-user evaluation, but the summary does not report statistical significance tests and relies on simulated users for much RL training. Overall the conclusions are supported but tempered by the independence assumption and some missing experimental details.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments comparing Soft-KB agents to appropriate baselines (Rule, Hard-KB, No-KB, RL) across multiple dataset sizes and using sensible success/reward metrics, including some real-user evaluation. The mathematical formulation — computing a posterior over KB rows by marginalizing independent per-slot belief distributions — is plausible and correctly described, but relies on the strong independence assumption the authors explicitly acknowledge. The experimental methodology is generally sound (proper baselines, reward design, dataset splits), though reliance on a user simulator for RL training and no mention of statistical significance or code release are minor gaps that do not fundamentally undermine the reported conclusions.

main_claims: Introduce a differentiable Soft-KB lookup that produces a posterior over KB entities from slot-wise belief distributions, replacing symbolic/hard KB queries.
Soft-KB lookup helps the agent discover better dialogue policies and enables end-to-end (online) training of both language understanding/belief trackers and the dialogue policy.
Integrating the Soft-KB lookup with reinforcement learning yields higher task success rates and higher reward in simulations and against real users.
Present a fully neural end-to-end KB-InfoBot trained entirely from user feedback.
_summary.methodology: Compute a probabilistic posterior over KB rows by marginalizing independent per-slot belief distributions (ptj) and knowledge indicators (qtj) via a Soft-KB lookup; implement belief trackers with GRUs (neural) or hand-crafted matching, and use a GRU-based neural policy trained with REINFORCE that samples R results from the KB posterior when informing the user.
baselines: Rule agents (hand-crafted belief tracker + hand-crafted policy)
RL agents (hand-crafted belief tracker + neural policy)
Hard-KB (hard lookup as in prior work)
metrics: dialogue success (user target in top-R results)
episodic reward (with rank-based reward: max(0,2(1-(r-1)/R)); failure reward -1; per-turn penalty -0.1)
limitations: Assumes independence of column/slot values (stated as a strong assumption).
Does not handle new entities or new relations at test time.
Training reinforcement learners requires simulated users (training is challenging and uses a user simulator).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dialogue systems (information access) / reinforcement learning
